steps.title: Compare the means
steps.content:
From the frequency table, the original total weight is
$$13(12)+14(10)+15(3)+16(5)+17(7)+18(12)+19(13)+20(9)=1{,}183$$
The original mean is
$$\frac{1{,}183}{71}$$
which is about $16.66$. After adding $32$, the new mean is
$$\frac{1{,}183+32}{72}=\frac{1{,}215}{72}$$
which is about $16.88$. So the mean increases.

steps.title: Compare the medians
steps.content: With $71$ values, the original median is the $36$th value. The cumulative count reaches $37$ at weight $17$, so the original median is $17$. After adding $32$, the new data set has $72$ values; the middle values are the $36$th and $37$th, which are still both $17$. The median stays the same. The answer is B.
